CL Wa igy@ :? r es ,4 Woes Saas va rr aad on SRT ars al Gn fee" weir Mp Diet she Makes owl tr erecta taal ' ; OF) rue ew pat ert oe he wearily arp, a et yssir : rey hagt: oaee =; have : io ' =e _ . i : Tao a . "te (4) omen eRe tea ~ (len Teeth ah ' ema? sy lharer) ery a ; | e itemadh hat t arPy z he wi = . a | im ees ies] -- Th, On we ei Py. | Th, OBSERVATIONS ON THE 90s | i TAV A i q a 'et ad ". @ Magazines: Sweepstakes and the Subscription Debacle Throughout this book you have no doubt savored a slew of magazine subscription efforts ranging from simple double postcards to the elegant mailings of Bill Jayme and Heikki Ratalahti (See chapter 31). Some of them require existing knowledge of the magazine; others draw you in and intrigue with you with the features and the benefits of subscribing. Theoretically, a brave new world for magazines should exist--what with the Internet and its worldwide reach. But it ain't so. The fact is, the world of magazines is in total disarray. The culprits: competition (a dizzying number of new magazines), sweepstakes offers, and incalculably lazy subscription managers. A quarter century ago, readers had a choice of around 1,300 titles as opposed to nearly 5,000 today. (This number does not include trade magazine published for individual industries.) Before spending money with a magazine, an advertiser wants to be absolutely sure that the ad will reach the num- ber of paid readers the publisher promises. This is called the "rate base." The word "paid" is important here; some- one who has paid for a subscription presumably has a vested interest in the magazine (and the subject area of the publication) and so is a desirable candidate for an advertiser. The Audit Bureau of Circulation (ABC) which monitors rate bases, decreed long ago that if a subscriber bought a magazine from a sweepstakes agency (Publishers Clearing House (PCH) or American Family Publishers (AFP) and paid the bill, it can be considered a paid subscription. It makes no never mind that the "subscriber" is really a gambler who has been promised winnings of $10 million in huge banner headlines which are qual- ified by mousetype that says if he has "the winning number and returns it" and the odds are million to 1. Sweepstakes buyers are traditionally dismal renewers. It's axiomatic that if you sell with the sweepstakes, you must bill with a sweepstakes and renew with a sweepstakes. They are a drug. The deal circulation directors made with PCH and AFP was simple: Gimme the names of paid subscribers so I can show my publisher who can then sell adver- tising. PCH and AFP kept most or all of the payments. At right: A disputed Publishers Clearing House sweepstakes effort. This is a toned-down effort that doesn't promise $10 million--the result of the avalanche of lawsuits from state attorneys general. The former president of Rodale Press, Bob Teuffel, points out that in terms of consumer benefits, these sweeps mailings offer consumers the lowest price for magazine subscriptions. In addition, by ganging up the offers, these are far more environmentally friendly than if each and every magazine sent out millions of individual offers. However in their desperation to save sagging responses, the sweepstakes marketers simply went beyond the pale in terms of making outrageous promises of huge winnings. MAGAZINES: SWEEPSTAKES AND THE SUBSCRIPTION DEBACLE The Ouick Fix--The Professional Discount Offer It made no difference that these "paid subscribers" were far more interested in winning $10 million than reading the magazine and the only reason they purchased a subscrip- tion was because they thought it enhanced their chances of winning. (It did not.) Nor did it matter to circulation direc- tors that the mailings got so sleazy in their deceptive prom- ises that gullible senior citizens actually went broke buying airline tickets in order to bring their "winning" entries to Ed ay a McMahon and Dick Clark in Florida. The new breed of cir- a es culation directors were venal number crunchers who cared little eR EE, : ee for the consumer or the image of their magazines. Finally, the Senate held hearings (I have video tapes and weasel- ing representatives from the sweeps marketers that would make your skin crawl). Further, state attorneys general sued and the sweeps companies paid gigantic fines and were forced to mend their ways. In the wake of all the catastrophic publicity, the pub- lic finally wised up and stopped responding to sweepstakes offers. Response to sweeps mailings tanked and magazines did not make their rate bases. Several, including LIFE, went out of business. According to Dan Capell of Capell's Circulation Report, in their heyday, sweepstakes offers accounted for 15% to 20% of all mag- azine subscriptions. By the turn of the century, they represented five to percent--"and probably more like five." Magazine publishers were suddenly faced with four choices: (1) rebate money to advertisers; (2) cut their rate base; (3) jump in the mail and get sub- scribers the old-fashioned way--on their own; (4) do nothing and hope nobody notices. BOULDER CO soz Number above is the logical choice. Unfortu- nately, most of these circulation directors had no idea how to work with writers and designers to create ben- efit-oriented circulation promotions. So many opted for the "quick fix"--the professional discount offer. In Capell's words, "The circulation directors are trying to cut the cost of the package rather than get new names." Oniginally this genre was sent to businesses, the idea being that this would be a good way for magazines to be seen in waiting rooms which, hopefully, would result in additional subscriptions from those who were Waiting. Then someone tested this format to straight consumer lists and it worked, as people felt flattered that someone thought they were "professionals" and were willing to offer big discounts. As you can see on the Newsweek lift piece at right (head- line: ATTENTION), the copy at the bottom reads: "THIS OFFER NOT AVAILABLE TO THE GENERAL PUBLIC." This is a transparent attempt to convey exclusivity. As Denison Mr. t Phila. The Importance of Arithmetic in Direct Mail Chart #1 Full-Dress Control Package vs. Postcard Test Gross Payment Net Net/PL Opening this book is a long section on using the % After yr double postcard to sell magazine subscriptions. Control 34% 27.00% 0.900% ---$4.436 Because the double postcard is cheaper in the mail Postcard 4.46% 20.50% 0.955% +$2,418 than the full-dress direct mail package--and it Diff from PC , ar Sn eee : pate a Pek einernaite questions: vs.Control +39.50% -24.10% +6.10% N/A The same could be said for the Professional Discount format--cheap, quick, easy. The answer lies in the concept of "lifetime value" Chart #2 which is the key element in the success or failure of a direct marketing business. It's okay to break even or lose money acquiring a customer (or subscriber or, in the case of charities, a donor) if that person Package v. Postcard--Cumulative Results from a Mailing of 100,000 Pieces er f : Control Postcard Control Postcard ai ates money with you over the coming P/L Net Subs Net Subs months and years. ceed consultant Gordon Grossman bDril- aes se ieee oe aye liantly described this concept in his Grossman on as ne As Baie poe Circulation--1994 where he shared the results of a es $26,556 020,200 Wo L260 five-year study comparing the lifetime value of sub- Year $33,617 $28,642 1,568 1,423 scribers to a consumer magazine who responded to 2€@ $38,870 $30,585 1,669 1,461 a double postcard vs. those who ordered as the _ Lifetime $53,803 $33,160 2,024 1,517 result of a full-dress direct mail package: Using Chart 1, an inexperienced direct marketer would scan the results of this test and conclude that the double post- card test was the clear winner. The offer, remember, was a bill-me, so gross response is meaningless. It is imperative to wait for the pay- ment history of this offer. And although the difference in net sub- scriptions is slightly higher for the double postcard (955 vs. for the control), the dollar profit/loss would indicate the double post- card was far more successful--a swing of $6,854. Theoretically, this is a no-brainer; the double postcard won hands down. Or did it? Take a look at Chart 2. Analyzed over five years--the first year pay-up, the second year conversion (a term used to describe a first year renewal), and three years of renewals--you discover that the full-dress package is the clear winner--both in terms of net subscriptions (507 more subs or +33.4%) and profit ($20,643 or +62.3%). Thus, a circulation director who scrapped the control in favor of the double postcard would be a big loser over the long term. What accounts for the difference? Very likely, the responders to the full-dress control package were readers who spent time studying the letter, circular, order form, lift pieces, etc. and were persuaded that this was a publication they would like to have come into their homes. Charts copyright Gordon W. Grossman 1995. All rights reserved. Reprinted with permission. MAGAZINES: SWEEPSTAKES AND THE SUBSCRIPTION DEBACLE The double postcard responders, on the other hand, were more likely skimmers and scanners, browsers and lookers. This model may well apply to the Professional Discount offer as well. Time will tell. Circumventing the Renewal Problem The magazine business has the most inferior circulation model of any publishing category--a business that regularly takes its cus- tomers out once a year, points a gun at them and says, "if you don't pay me right now, I'm no longer shipping you any product." Renewal notices are a coping mechanism for a fatally flawed selling system. No other service in the world forces its customers to reevaluate its usefulness once a year and decide whether or not to continue. Newspapers would be out of business if this were the model. --Jay Walker, Founder NewSub Services Jay Walker has been wrong about a lot of things in his life--most notably his tectonic fiasco, Priceline.com. However, in the area of magazine subscriptions, he is right on. Being forced to renew a publication is a nui- sance. Ed Lind, my Philadelphia Inquirer delivery person doesn't ring my doorbell once a year and ask me if I want to renew. He simply delivers the paper and, once a month, includes a bill. The same is true for The Wall Street Journal and The New York Times. If I weren't forced to think through the worth of magazines to which I had sub- scribed, I probably would still be receiving such losers as Talk, Brill's Content, and The New York Observer. A number of magazines circumvent this problem by calling themselves membership organizations--The National Geographic, Smithsonian, and Natural History, for exam- ple. According to the late guru, Dick Benson, if you are a membership organiza- tion with the publication being the main benefit of membership, you are allowed under FTC rules to simply bill for dues rather than be forced to beg for a renewal. Benson said this scheme will raise renewal rates. In Benson's words (from his Secrets of Successful Direct Mail): "Memberships renew better than plain subscriptions by 10% or more." MILLION DOLLAR MarLinc$ Automatic Renewals For as long as I can remember, the subscription model that maga- zine publishers drooled over was that of Travel @ Leisure and, later, Food @ Wine--both published by American Express and automati- cally renewed once a year with a small line item on the cardmem- ber's bill. Usually the charge was buried deep amidst huge T&E expenses and was so relatively insignificant that it was paid with- out thinking about it. As a result, both magazines--editorially ordi- nary by any standard--enjoy phenomenally high renewal rates. In his fascinating and useful Grossman on Circulation--1995, former Reader's Digest circulation director and magazine consultant Gordon Grossman wrote: For at least as long as I've been in this business (which is now years), American publishers have talked about the potential advantages of using automatic renewal offers. They have looked longingly at colleagues in the United Kingdom and Holland, where automatic renewal is the standard method of selling sub- scriptions. Many tests have been tried. Most of the tests that I'm aware of have not "failed" in the traditional way--they just got so garbled in fulfillment that they can't be read. Five years later (2000), Grossman said, "Automatic renewal is the biggest thing in circulation right now. Everybody who's doing it is making money. Time, Inc., Rodale, Reader's Digest--are able to move response rates up a bit and cut renewal costs." He added, "But to make it work, it must be done right and done right from the start." Here is how Time magazine phrased the automatic renewal offer on AOL: HOW YOUR FREE TRIAL/SPECIAL GIFT OFFER WORKS: Your Trial Issues are truly risk-free. If you don't like TIME, no problem. You can cancel at any time within the trial period by calling the number we'll provide. The issues will be yours to keep, FREE. If you like what you see, do nothing. TIME will send more issues and charge you (after the trial period) at the low price of just $24.98 for a total of issues. Plus, with this special offer, you'll get a matching set of His & Hers TIME Elite Watches. After that, TIME will automatically renew your subscription every six months using your current billing method you use to pay for your AOL account. As an added bonus, you'll always get the guaran- teed low price then available. Your satisfaction is always guaran- teed and you can cancel at any time. You will receive a full refund on any unmailed issues. By clicking "ORDER," I am asking America Online to give TIME all required information about the billing method I currently use to pay for AOL and to authorize TIME to charge my subscription to that account. TIME is published weekly, except for occasional combined, extra or expanded issues. Subscribers: If the Post Office alerts us that your magazine is undeliverable, we have no further obliga- tion unless we receive a corrected address within two years. MAGAZINES: SWEEPSTAKES AND THE SUBSCRIPTION DEBACLE Yet Another Wrinkle Enter Jay Walker with a fascinating new concept that is saving at least some of the bacon of those magazines that had relied too heavily on the sweepstakes marketers. With his NewSub services, which Walker co-founded with publishing wizard Michael Loeb (son of Fortune editor Marshall Loeb), consumers are offered a list of mag- azines as stuffers in monthly credit card statements. Unlike the sweepstakes mailings, where each magazine is represented by a stamp and to subscribe, you paste it on the sweeps entry form, NewSub Services simply offers a list of magazines at great prices. Choose one or more of the titles, and your subscription is automatically charged to the credit card every six months on a til- forbid basis. (You will continue to receive the magazine and your card will continue to be charged every six months unTIL you FORBID it--in other words, until you cancel.) In another, equally inventive scheme, Walker has cut a deal with major airlines whereby they furnish NewSub with a list of frequent fliers and the number miles that will be expiring from non-use. Simply check off the magazines you want to subscribe to, and you pay for them with miles. As program director Carolyn Mott writes: "Magazines are available for as few as miles and there's no cash cost." This is the NewSub Services self-mailer that goes out to frequent fliers whose miles are expiring. Thirty- nine magazines are offered for as little as miles (Spin) and as much as 5,500 miles (Daily Variety). On both top panels--of the letter and the reply device at right, the available award miles are listed, and the salutation is personalized. Many frequent fliers do not accrue enough miles on an individual airline to garner any kind of free travel benefits. This is the next best thing. The frequent flier benefits, because the miles are not wasted; the magazine benefits, because the consumer has paid something (miles, not cash) which satisfies the Audit Bureau of Circulation that this is a paid subscriber; and, of course, NewSub Services wins. Postcard letter: 51/2" x 41/2", unfolds to 5!/2"x 13". soto MAGAZINES: SWEEPSTAKES AND THE SUBSCRIPTION DEBACLE The Legendary "J" Mailings The "magazine tear sheet" at right with the yel- low Post-It™ arrived in a plain white #10 enve- lope with my name typed on it and a First Class Stamp. It is truly startling. When I first received it, I thought, "Who the hell is J?" I first reported on this format way back in January, when it was dreamed up by little- known West Coast Investment advisor, John Pugsley, who self-published a book, Common Sense Economics, that wasn't selling. In that itera- tion, it arrived as a folded up tabloid newspaper sheet printed in magic marker on newsprint with the message: Dennis Great offer! R. Since then, this technique has been used by several mailers, notably entrepreneurs Jay Abraham and Ted Nicholas who used it to sell his Secrets of Self-Publishing book. Instead of using a magic marker directly on the page, Nicholas used a Post-It™. According to Nicholas, if you have a successful ad targeted to a narrow market, this would work. Needed: e A white, closed-faced envelope with person's name and address hand typed e A first class stamp Advertisement Applause, Applau: Anyone can win over a tough Linn, speechwriter. Just ask Lee hte Try tha es, THE AMERICAN SPEAKER Your Guide to Successful Speaking Aram Bakshian, Jr., Editor (600 pages; Georgetown Publishing House) American Speaker. By Len Taylor Bakshian, Jr., in this remarkable new resource for public speakers, is the ability to communicate clearly and effec- tively. Never has this been more true than in today's intensely competitive business climate. Bakshian should know. Speechwriter to "The Great Com- municator" himself, Ronald Reagan, as well as to two other for- mer presidents and the heads of several major corporations, Bakshian has witnessed the rise and fall of international leaders based on their ease -- or lack of ease -- on the podium. Anyone can master the art of speaking in public, Bakshian says, "In the last analysis, the spoken word is still king." Fear and loathing of the rubber chick- en circuit have long plagued public fig- ures. "No one knows how I hate T= difference between success and failure, writes Aram making speeches," President Calvin Coolidge once complained to a friend Bakshian tackles head-on the chal- lenges of public speaking in AMERICAN Sreaker. "As with alcoholism," he writes, "there is no known cure for stage fright You're either a 'chronic' sufferer or a 'recovering' sufferer." In either case, it's J easy to minimize that suffering -- or even turn it into an advantage. As Carroll O'Connor, the legendary "Archie Bunker," put it, "A professional actor has a kind of tension. The amateur is thrown by it, but the professional needs it." Perhaps the best contemporary exam- ple is Lee lacocca, who saved the Chrysler Corporation by using his enormous talent as a speaker to win the support of the Congress, the White House and the American people for the biggest corporate bailout in history. Iacocca himself attributes his business success to speaking. In his autobiography, he writes: "I've seen a lot of guys who are smarter than I am and a lot who know more about cars. And yet I've lost The chairmen of Fortui IBM and General Mot them in the smoke. Why? Because I'm tough? No ... You've got to know how to talk to them, plain and simple." Business is the single biggest rhetorical arena. From simple retail sales spiels to sensitive boardroom presentations, speech keeps the wheels of commerce turning. In making a first impres- sion, Bakshian writes, "Your appearance can raise expectations, but what you say and how you say it will determine how people evaluate you." A good speaker is always in demand. At events from business conventions to weddings, "a good speaker not only adds to the occasion, he also benefits from 'free advertising' that adds to his stature in the community and attracts future business." than most politicians do. And They speak all the time in the workplace and to col- leagues, customers and the media. with ht public antidot speakit and-an F a r every occasion, from the Thanksgiving toast to a defense of your industry before a hostile audience, Arranged alphabetically, AMERICAN SPEAKER is easy to navi- gate, highly entertaining and loaded with good ideas. In the cal- endars section, for instance, Bakshian compiles thousands of speech pegs for every day of the year in three calendars: celebri- SPRAKE & | e design: | = ly, ane > a It's Ee : i ty birthdays, today in history and the months at a glance. "Every audience gathered to share a common interest or celebrate a spe- cific occasion has a built-in common bond," Bakshian writes. "A good speaker doesn't just know this; a good speaker takes advantage of it." He demonstrates how a shared reference can warm up the audience, draw a favorable analogy or build a bridge from past to present. What about actual speeches? They're all over AMERICAN SPEAKER. A section on acceptance speeches includes as an exam- M@ ple Winston Churchill's masterful appearance before Parliament in 1954, on the occasion of his 80th birthday. To illus- § trate the business address, Bakshian quotes nine speeches that used humor and anecdotes to deliver serious mes- sages to several very different audiences. In the education section, Bakshian shows how cartoonist Garry Trudeau hilarious- ly defused the "political correctness" time bomb in speaking to a graduating class at Yale University. And so on. But here's what really makes AMERICAN SPEAKER stand out from the crowd of business publi- cations. In addition to the basic 600-page volume, readers also receive timely updates, transcripts of recent, powerful speeches and a free consulting service with Bakshian, to resolve those last- minute speaking challenges. Best of all, the entire package is guaranteed. Review AMERICAN SPEAKER for days. If it doesn't meet your expectations, return it to Georgetown Publishing House for a complete refund just on television Few professionals can afford to ignore a promise like that AMERICAN SPEAKER ($297, including bimonthly updates) is not available in any bookstore. Copies are available only from Georgetown Publishing House To order, write to: Georgetown Publishing House, 30th St. NW, Dept. 4KE6, Washington, DC 20007. Or call them at 800-915-0022 -- or, fax 202-337-1512. e A full-page newspaper ad on newsprint with stock market tables on the back (so it seems like a real tear sheet) ¢ The page must have a ragged edge so it appears to be torn out. e A hand-written Post-It™; it cannot be preprinted in the person's handwriting and filled in. It must be the real thing. In the early 90s, Georgetown Publishing president Dani Levinas used it to sell subscriptions to American Speaker. The Georgetown version arrived looking like a page torn from a news or current events magazine and was mailed in the millions--as an unbeatable control for years. Enter the FTC Levinas agreed to stop mailing the package when the FTC charged that he misled consumers by sending them what appeared to be an independent book review torn from a magazine and tricked the prospect into thinking it had been sent by a friend named "J". Levinas made tweaks (e.g., running the word "ADVERTISEMENT" on the top of the page) and other changes. Who is "J"? Quite simply, "J" is the quickest, easiest script letter to write. In the years I published Who's Mailing What! this format brought in the most reader mail. You'd think the FTC would have better things to do. MILLION DOLLAR MAILING$ That Curious Bastard, the Magalog The original premise of Million Dollar Mailings was to examine long-term controls--mailings that were received for three or more consecutive years. The logic: If it worked for three years straight, it was a moneymaker. The fallacy of this blueprint was that certain formats that lent themselves to constant change were ignored. Example: Sweepstakes. If you send out the very same sweepstakes mailing for three straight years, people would assume it was an old sweeps offer and the prize had long ago been won. So sweeps offers necessarily had to be constantly refurbished. If nothing else, the date the prize was to be awarded had to change. Another format that lends itself to constant change is the so-called magalog--part catalog, part magazine, part direct mail pitch--that has become a staple of the publishing industry, especially books, book clubs, and newsletters, particularly those in the fields of investments and health. The need for constant change is obvious. Just as no magazine editor could imag- ine putting the same cover on the newsstand month after month, no marketer would con- tinue to send out a magalog with the same cover over and over. The recipient would say, "Oh, I've seen this before," and consign it to the round file. It All Began with the Long, Long Letter Back in the 1970s and 80s, the traditional direct mail letter ran three or four pages of type- writer type. However, in the area of newsletters that offered investment advice, the premier copywriter was Long Island freelancer Gary Bencivenga who said, "The more you tell, the more you sell." The result, letters began to get longer and longer. Interspersed with all the bene- fits of subscribing to a particular publication, direct mail writers began dropping in little nuggets of information. One of the prime purveyors of financial newsletters in the early 1980s was Florida entre- preneur Joel Nadel whose publications had a wonderfully authentic European aroma. For example, he founded The Royal Society of Liechtenstein, a secret organization of interna- tional investors and entrepreneurs with the main benefit being a monthly newsletter. The Florida BBB shut it down after determining that it was neither royal nor a society nor had any- thing to do with Liechtenstein, whereupon it was bought by Bill Bonner of Agora (publisher of the hugely successful International Living newsletter) and renamed The Oxford Club. A splendid example of the long, long letter and Nadel's splendid trickery was this #10 effort. Nadel's blue envelope with red and blue type for his newsletter, CONFIDENTIAL: ZURICH REPORT, positively reeks of the old world with its interna- tional air mail border, German writing, return address in Germanic font, and the red wax seal on the flap of the envelope; all give it a sense of value and importance and an aura of mystery. However, there are two fascinating points. 1) Axel Andersson found grammatical mistakes in the German on the envelope. 2) The metered indicia states it was mailed from Zip Code 25430, or Kearneysville, Wv--a long way from the arms of the Gnomes of Zurich! The 16-page letter has the following copy in big type at the top: EMERGENCY BULLETIN!. Dangerous economic developments imminent. U.S. Media coverage biased, incomplete, and misleading. Could ruin American investors. Immediate action required to safeguard assets and investments. "Low profile" report offers Seco tCmS Olu elomcr The courier font makes it appear to have been com- posed on a typewriter. The letter is filled with news sto- ries and fascinating predictions of upcoming financial doom and gloom--plus bright promises of how a sub- scriber can not only weather the storm but make money while all other investors are losing their shirts. Adding to the caché is the reply envelope which is addressed to a P.O. Box in Zurich (subscriber supplies a 44¢ stamp). The offer here is terrific: LL} YES--Please begin my introductory subscription to CONFIDENTIAL: REPORT FROM ZURICH and send me the FREE bonus report, The Swiss Solution. Also send me your private 24-hour Swiss Insiders' hotline phone number which I may use as often as I wish for the entire term of my subscription. One Year--12 monthly issues for $69 (Reg. $138 plus bonuses worth $444). Let me say that I was delighted to have unearthed and read again this wonderfully hokey mailing. I have not seen a format like this--with all its beguilement, sense of conspiracy, and European-ness--for years. Call me a sucker, but I think it's really neat. And I remember receiving a slew of these, so it must have worked. I have got to believe that some entrepreneur could go back to the well and, with an updated message, make this work all over again. What would the updated message be? Given the roller coaster ride that dot-com investors took in 2000, a pitch from Zurich or Liechtenstein or Antwerp offering the security of precious metals acer or diamonds might just fly. =o yor yorrang g a WEA SSSA SSNR SSSA SSRE ~SAA Hans Geldmeister Zurich, Switserland PERSONAL AND CONFIDENTIAL Denison Road Stamford USA Bans Seldmeister BO. Box C€H.-8023 Zurich, Switzerland EMERGENCY BULLETIN!...Dangerous economic developments imminent. U.S. media coverage biased, incomplete and misleading. Could ruin American investors. Immediate action required to safeguard assets and investments. "Low profile" report offers strategic solutions. Dear Friend; Right now, nine global developments are threatening your nest egg...your financial security...your investments, your privacy...even your family's well-being. Here is the first and perhaps the most important warning you'll read this year -- one that, we believe, will affect everything you've ever worked for... BEHIND-THE-SCENES MANIPULATORS ARE SETTING UP THE STOCK MARKET FOR THE BIGGEST CRASH SINCE 1929. Don't be fooled by all the fuss over the Dow passing and the predictions of another to years of prosperity. Today's cheap oil prices and low interest rates have created the false expectation of continued low inflation. Hence the present bull market in stocks -- which is exactly what the two powerful manipulators want: the Saudis will ultimately have the control of the oil marketplace that they want. And the politicians will have created the appearance of a healthy economy to serve their purposes at election time. By the end of this year, the market may go as high as 2800. (In the market reached new highs also.) By late the Dow will top 3000, but that will prove disastrous for thousands of unsuspecting investors. That's because after the elections, the bottom will fall out. Suddenly, low oil prices and the government's free-wheeling monetary policy will come to a screaming halt and drive interest rates up sharply, These two combined forces will put a tremendous inflationary pressure on the economy and set off the biggest stock market crash since 1929! This inflation will affect everyone, particularly those at or near retirement age... Warning #2: IMMINENT DOUBLE-DIGIT INFLATION WILL MAKE RETIREMENT IMPOSSIBLE POR MANY CURRENTLY PROSPEROUS INDIVIDUALS. Someone--probably Gary Bencivenga--came up with the idea of simply taking the long letter, together with an order form, a lift piece, and brochure-like element and turning them all into a self-mailer with a reply envelope bound in it. At right and below are the cover and pages from a magalog for Taipan, a brainchild of entrepreneur Bill Bonner who built Agora Publishing huge increases in farm ouiput) in others. and disasirous hot, dry aummers ahead for much of the U.S. see page into an $80 million international mini-conglom- erate with offices in Baltimore, London, and a Chateau in the French countryside. Instead of an outside carrier envelope with teaser copy, the cover of this self-mailer is its own teaser. Nothing for the reader to open; rather you simply start reading when you come to it in your pile of morning mail. Note Bonner's titillating subhead: Major Events of the 1990s that will Change Life Forever After finishing reading the cover, you turn the page and discover that Bonner's 17-page let- ter begins with "Dear reader" at the bottom of page 2. He moves from a Times or Tames = Roman font to a typewriter font, thus giving the letter a more personal feel--as though he composed it on his Remington electric. Here is direct mail letter writing at its best (Bonner is arguably the world's greatest copywriter) with a series of short paragraphs, indented para- graphs, asterisked paragraphs--all designed with the intention of keeping the eye moving. What is it that enabled this mailing to beat a control? Presumably the lists were the same so logic would dictate that its success was based on the offer and, to some extent, the design. The early magalogs worked, so the creators began coming up with more and more sophisti- cated designs. As magalogs grew more sophisticated, so too did the marketing philosophy behind them. Pioneer Gary Bencivenga likens magalogs to "infomercials in print. They explain and demonstrate the product; the offer testimonials." He added: "Magalogs remind me of the old General Foods salesman who used to go door-to-door bringing a free scoop of coffee for housewives to try. They'd say 'try this, and I'll be back next week to take your order.' Magalogs serve the same pur- pose, by giving readers a free scoop of the product." The top illustration, a maga- log for Agora's Strategic Investment offering the advice of gurus James Dale Davidson and Lord William Rees-Mogg, is one of the early ver- sions that used photographs. Of the prospectus effort, Phillips Publishing's designer Ed Elliott said back in 1993: "The magalogs we're mailing today fall into two categories--the tradi- tional, consumer-magazine style and the report style. For the finan- cial publications, we have been having some success mailing the report-style magalog--and its varia- tions such as the prospectus style we recently tested for Young's Intelligence Report. He recalled how his former boss, David Ogilvy, used to say that when you're creating space advertising, you want to make the ad look like an article. "The magalog does for direct mail what the editorial-look did for space. That's why it's been such a strong innovation in direct mail." In the upper right corner is the following, which gives it the per- ceived value of a magazine or special report: $5 U.S. $6 Canada The center spread of this magalog has the look and feel of a mag- azine with sidebars and illustrations. At the same time, the business reply envelope is bound in, which tells the reader that some action is expected. ~ The clarion call for the action comes in the inside back spread (shown at left). Here is a description of the premiums on the left and the order coupon at right along with the money-back guarantee. Plenty of order options: cash with order, credit card, or telephone. Note the word "FREE" plastered all over the place, along with two extra premiums for a two-year subscription. When this magalog format first appeared, it beat envelope mail- ings hands down. "It was intended to disarm people," Ed Elliott told me recently. "This was no typical junk mail, but rather it appeared to be something valuable." But after a while, that approach seemed to die. As more and more investment marketers mailed them, Elliott suggested, "they became the new definition of junk mail." As a result, the format and copy got more and more sophisticated in the coming years. THAT CURIOUS BASTARD, THE MAGALOG The Two Faces of the Modern Magalog SPECIAL GREATEST SECRETS EDITION e Face 1: The Soft Offer Magalogs are a different breed (and read) than the old direct mail package that arrived in a carrier envelope. Yet according to designer William Fridrich, the basic "&) Five "@ Nears elements remain. The magazine-like cover replaces the outside envelope. Inside, you'll generally find a letter; although it is no longer the main salesman, it serves to tell the reader that this product has been created by a real person and not a bunch of faceless editors. The other traditional elements--brochure, lift piece, testimonials, guarantee, order mechanism, reply enve- lope--are all part of the magalog. But the added dimension is the perceived value of editorial content. As Phillips Publishing's Ed Elliott says, "I can do more with a self-mailer (magalog) that resembles an editorial product that I cannot do with a traditional letter. "First of all, don't need to get somebody to open it, because it's not in an envelope. In effect, it's already open." Your Money Every Even if Wall Street CRASHES! oe. All Yours Yo Use FREE...F You Act How -- gf See This Invitation Again! sy INSIDE: 1,267 el Agents Have Declared OFF-LIMITS! This is true. The prospect can start reading right away. Lawyers, Doctors Account However, the covers are so startling and the headlines so powerful (and so memorable) that once a prospect has seen it, it would not be forgotten. As a result, ifa marketer keeps mailing the same magalog over and over, it will be dis- carded; "I've seen this," prospects will say, even though they may never have opened it. The 16-page Boardroom magalogs--or self-mailers--you see here are all identical on the inside. The top three are enormous, measuring 10" x 13!/2" and printed on glossy paper in blazing full color. The smaller version at right was mailed for two years during 1997-1998, whereupon it was expanded to tabloid size and given these new covers during and 2000. This is a huge departure from the long-term control for Bottom Line/Personal and Book of Inside Information efforts you see on pages 377-383. Incidentally, Marty Edelston of Boardroom jeal- ously guarded the identity of his ace copywriter, Mel Martin, so his name was not revealed in the previous edition. When Mel died, Marty asked me to write a tribute to this genius with words who, literally, put Boardroom into the top ranks of newsletter publish- ers. I wrote it for Target Marketing, and it later appeared in my book, Method Marketing (also pub- PARTIAL TABLE OF CONTENTE weal re " The BEST investment Secrets To Hetp You Cash in Big -- "ven If The Stock Market Crashes. } The BEST Tux Tactics That Will Help You Legally Reduce Your Tax Bill To ZERO. @- The BEST Health Seorets -- Near Miracutous New Cures _ That Heal Your Body And Cast Abrast Nothing. BEST Retirement Secrets That Guarantee With Dignity--With More Money Than You W Frer Need. Plus, The BEST Travel Secrets That Help You Fly Safer, More Comfortably And Far Cheaper. A wcnhentes inher ah Bsn lished by Bonus Books, Inc.). Wild Graphics = Soft Offer According to Ed Elliott, the wild and crazy graphics and relatively light copy you see here (and on equivalent magalogs for Rodale Press) work well on soft (or bill-me) offers. Phillips, on the other hand, uses copy-intensive magalogs, coupled with a huge array of free gifts or premi- ums, but always demands either cash with order or a credit card charge. MILLION DOLLAR Matinc$ On these facing pages, you see two competing direct mail philosophies that very likely appeal to two different audiences. The Boardroom-Rodale readers are people looking for a quick fix while Phillips serves customers hungry for information who are willing to pony up cash on the burled for it. Face 2: The Hard Offer (Phillips/Agora) Designer Welliam Fridrich, an early pioneer of magalog formats and the first to take it to tabloid size, says his aim is "to give enough information so they can't open it over the trash, but rather put this puppy aside for further reading. I've sat on air- planes and watched people take one of my maga- logs out of a briefcase and read it cover to cover just like a magazine." Phillips' Ed Elliott firmly believes that an enve- lope package is a linear reading experience. Although you have various elements--letter, circular, lift piece, order device--they must be read one at a time. The subheads on a letter, for example, are not as compelling as sidebars, pull quotes, testimonials, and other bells and whistles found in a magalog. When prospects flip through the magalog, they are given "many more points of entry. Every spread gives a brand new opportunity to engage some- body who has not started reading." In effect, every spread equals the teaser of an envelope. The original magalogs--first tested in the early 1990s--were intended to disarm the prospect. Clearly, these were not junk mail, but rather some- thing valuable. Once out there to the investment market, said Elliott, they did fabulously. But after, they became the new definition of junk mail and started to die. So the designers switched them to look more like a special report and, later, a tabloid. The Phillips magalog you see here started out with the "Common Mistakes" headline shown at the top. The interior fulfilled the promise on the cover with chapter-like sections devoted to each area of health--cancer, heart, prostate, arthritis, etc. "But three-year-old messages to a limited universe don't cut it," Elliott said. So the covers were changed while the inside remained the same. The next itera- tion was headlined, "Do You Take Any of These Popular Vitamins or Supplements" and, later, eIne Great Vitamin Hoax." Both worked just as well as the prior headline. Phillips believed it brought in younger consumers who were vitamin buyers. Answers _¢: crsntomestncneenscemn us ocemncc ean et THAT CURIOUS BASTARD, THE MAGALOG Tested as a 76-page so-called bookalog (5!/2" x 81/2") under the title, M.D. 's Digest, it depressed response by 40%. So it was expanded to look like a newspaper (101/2" x 13") and response popped back up to the original pace. At pages, the mailing cost was less. Elliott's advice: If you can find a good strong control, change for- mat and appearance as well as headlines and lead-ins, and you can keep the thing alive for a long time. Unlike the graphically explosive model across the page of Boardroom and Rodale, these copy-heavy efforts ask for cash (or credit card) with order. For that reason, they are offer- heavyy--with a total of free special reports if you order (and pay for) a two-year subscription. In the words of the late guru Dick Benson, "Free is a magic word." And on the ordering spread shows at right, FREE appears some times. Is your offer a bill-me or cash-with-order? The soft offer tradi- tionally brings a higher up-front response. But you have to go through all the hassle of billing series and bad debt. In direct mail, arithmetic is king (and queen). You test. MILLION DOLLAR Matuinc$ I have never really been comfortable with fund raising. The rea- son is that I have done a great deal of reporting on the practices of fund raisers and have come to the conclusion that a good per- cent of national organizations that raise money by mail are in business to make money for themselves and the causes they pro- fess to help would be far better off if these charlatans were totally out of the picture. An example. Peggy and I have taken three photographic safaris to Kenya and Tanzania. We love to watch the animals under the giant sky of the Serengeti and the Mara. During a grim period in the 1980s, Somali ivory poachers would cross the border into Kenya and kill ele- phants for their tusks (and, occasionally, kill tourists who got in their way). Several wildlife organizations were in the field protecting ele- phants: African Wildlife, Wildlife Conservation International (funded by the Bronx Zoo), and, of course, the Kenya Wildlife people under the direction of Richard Leakey. They raised money for their work by mail and their revenues went to protecting elephant. At the same time, a number of phony-baloney, shyster organiza- tions rented lists of known animal lovers and begged for money to save African elephants. They had no operatives in Africa. They had no lobbyists in Washington and around the world working to ban the importation and sale of ivory. In fact, their usual business was raising money to save other animals. But using gory photographs of dead bloody elephants and heart-rending copy that hit the emo- tional hot buttons of fear (extinction of elephants), guilt (if you don't give, you will be responsible for this great tragedy), anger (at the Somali poachers), flattery (I'm writing you because you care), and salvation (elephants will be saved) they pried tons of money out of gullible, good-hearted Americans. What were their programs to save elephants? They were "raising the consciousness" of consumers about the problem. How? By mak- ing mailings to raise money to make mailings to raise money to make (ialungs tO... . The IRS and other regulatory agencies allow mail order charities to attribute a huge percentage of their mailing costs to "public edu- cation." As a result, the average consumer cannot begin to tell if a The Fund Raising Arena charity is legitimate or not. I'm an expert; I can't tell unless I have the charity's annual report, the Form #990 (which is filed with the IRS), and samples of the mailings in my hand. And even then, their charlatanism can be concealed. What happened with the elephants? People would receive one of these dishonest tearjerker mailings and mail off a check to save the elephants. Whereupon the next day a legitimate appeal to save the elephants would be received. "I gave to elephants yesterday," the beleaguered consumer would say. "I'll give to something else today." So elephants were hurt rather than helped by these mail order thugs. Peggy and I sent Richard Leakey $500 and urged that the money go for ammunition to shoot poachers. Leakey wrote back to say that it had. The dishonest save-the-elephants scenario is repeated across all categories of mail order charities--social action, environment, health and handicapped, international relief, minority, religion, children--you name it, and somebody is getting rich on the backs of the unfortunate they claim to be helping. To find out if the charity you are giving to is legit or not, the key number is the percentage of their programs earmarked for "public education." If the amount is in double digits, don't give. The only exception: Mothers Against Drunk Driving (MADD) who make no secret of the fact that they are trying to change behavior by making mailings. Give to MADD, and they promise to make more mailings and raise public consciousness. MILLION DOLLAR Marinc$ Easter Seals (One of the Good Guys) The following background material is from the Easter Seals Web site. Q: What does Easter Seals do? A: Easter Seals provides services to help children and adults with disabilities gain greater independ- ence so they can achieve their goals. Q: What kinds of services are provided by Easter Seals? A: Easter Seals children's services include early intervention and developmental preschool programs designed to assist young children with disabilities adapt and find ways to succeed in school and in their lives. Preschool and child development centers for children of all abilities offer families a safe, educa- tional environment, staffed by child care profession- als. After-school care programs offer extended learning opportunities. Easter Seals camping pro- grams give children and adults with and without dis- abilities the chance to become more independent. _ And respite services benefit children with disabilities and their families. Easter Seals vocational training - and employment services provide young people and adults with disabilities a full spectrum of services. Skills training, school-to-work transitioning, job placement, supported employment, assistive tech- nology/job accommodation, and mentoring pro- - grams help people with disabilities find and keep jobs that enhance independence and inclusion in the community. These are services offered at Easter Seals organizations across the country. Easter Seals con- tinues to be one of the nation's largest networks for physical medicine and rehabilitation services, offer- ing physical, occupational, and speech therapy serv- ices, as well as many other specialty services such as home health, aquatic therapy, pulmonary rehabili- tation, and low vision assistance. In addition, Easter Seals offers several support programs for spinal cord injuries, stroke, post-polio, and disabilities that occur as a part of aging. Q: Are Easter Seals services limited to certain types of disabilities? A: No. It doesn't make any difference what kind of disability a person has. Easter Seals provides disabil- ity screening, rehabilitation and support services to persons of all ages with disabilities resulting from any cause--disease, disability at birth, accidental injuries, and the aging process. Q: How many people receive Easter Seals services each year? A: During 1996-1997, Easter Seals programs served 1,049,913 people. In addition, more than one million people participated in Easter Seals pub- lic health education activities. Through professional education programs, Easter Seals also helped physi- cians, therapists, and other professionals learn ways to improve services for people with disabilities. Q: How did Easter Seals get started? A: The forerunner of today's organization was founded in by Edgar Allen, an Ohio business- man, after his son was killed in a streetcar accident. The lack of adequate medical services to save his son's life motivated him to organize the building of a hospital in his community. Subsequently, he took a deep interest in a young boy who came to the hos- pital for orthopedic treatment. This experience made him aware of the needs of other children with disabilities. With the help of Rotary Clubs he formed the Ohio Society for Crippled Children, the first organization created to help children with physical disabilities. The movement gradually spread throughout the United States. In 1944, the words "and adults" were added to the National Society for Crippled Children's name because serving adults had become an increasingly important part of the organization's program. There were three more sig- nificant name changes. In 1967, the words "Easter Seal" were incorporated in the organization's name, reflecting the public's awareness and acceptance of the traditional campaign symbol. In 1979, the phrase "for crippled children and adults" was dropped because the term "crippled" had a negative connotation for people with and without disabilities and was no longer acceptable in the rehabilitation field. In 1998, "society" was dropped and an "s" was added to "seal:" Easter Seals. THE FuND RAISING ARENA The Five-Year Easter Seals . Z payee Seal Society Control i sag me - The 37/8" x 77/3" outer envelope has purple type and black lines. The name and address showing through the lower window are printed in black over a laven- der tint. Showing through the small back window are the signature full-color flower Easter Seals stamps-- two sheets of stamps each. These are known as "freemiums"--free goodies included in the mailing (as opposed to premiums which are goodies that will be sent when you order or contribute). These are meant to lay a guilt trip on the prospect. ("Gee, they sent me these pretty stamps; how can I not send some money.") The stamps are then used to decorate outgoing envelopes which means free publicity for the organization. The Courtesy Reply Envelope (sender pays the postage) has a space for the donor to affix one of the stamps. The order form contains a picture of the little girl and a note indicating how much she would like the person to give. | The back of the order form clearly shows how the money is used and the percentages are excellent: only 5.0% for public education and 76.5% for direct services to people with dis- i abilities. The 9.3 % for fund raising and 9.2% for management ATTACH and general expenses means Easter Seals is a group anyone __YOUR EASTER _ can feel good about. The Easter Seals Five-Year Control Main Letter Freelance direct mail writer Harry B. Walsh describes what is going on in this letter--a masterpiece that lays fear and guilt on to prospect's shoulders with a trowel. The tone of a good direct mail letter is as direct and personal as the writer's skill can make it. Even though it may go to millions of people, it never orates to i] Dear Friend, I ust confess that years ago didn't give as much thought as per- haps | should have to people with disabilities -- or to Raster Seals, whose miss! jon is to help people with disabilities achieve independence. That was before our daughter Mandi was born. Just three weeks prior to her birth, we were told she would have spina bifida -- which would leave her paralyzed from the hips down, wearing a body brace and leg braces, We already had two healthy daughters, so the news came as quite a shock. Looking back, realize our reaction as parents was typical. Grief, anguish -- éven anger. A feeling of "How can this happen to us? This happens only to other people!" We loved Haster Seal a crowd but rather murmurs into a single ear. It's a message from nelp Mandi one letter writer to one letter reader. Tell a story if possible. Everybody loves a good story, be it about Peter Rabbit or King Lear. And the direct mail letter with its unique person-to-person format, is the perfect vehicle for a story. Stories get read. The letter I wrote to launch the Cousteau Society 20-some years ago has survived hundreds of tests against it . The original of this direct mail Methuselah started out with the lead: "A friend once told me a curious story I would like to share with you..." Most consultants and writers teach that a letter should begin with offer and benefits that should be restated at least three times in the course of the letter along with several calls to action. However, in the fund-raising arena, all bets are off. How can you not give to Easter Seals after reading Debbie Rutherford's piteous story? It doesn't matter that the letter is five or seven years old and Mandi is probably a freshman in college. This is a highly per- sonal, emotional message that tugs at the heart stings. "There, but for the Grace of God, goes my little daugh- ter," you say to yourself as you bring out the checkbook. The main thing to remember in creating fundrais- ing letters: be emotional. Really tug at the heart- strings. In the words of freelancer Jack Maxson: "More than fifty percent of our buying decisions are based on emotion." And John J. Flieder said, "When reason and emo- tion come into conflict, emotion wins every time." About Freemiums The late guru Dick Benson advocated adding elements to your direct mail package; chances are you will increase response. Freemiums--such as the stamps you see here or the little pressure-sensitive personalized return address labels--have proved effective, especially in the fund raising arena. Mandi and many thousands of America are living proof of the value of Baster Seal services, programs and advocacy on their behalf. The fact is, there will always be disabilities, They can occur at any age, from birth, illness or accident. learn everything possible about spina bifida and the extent of Mandi's disability, And they worked | patiently -- day after day, hour after hour--to | What a difference this wonderful organization has made in our lives! Through Easter special therapy programs and one-on-one attention and support, Mandi's abilities have developed far beyond our highest expectations. She has become so independent that it brings tears of Joy to my eyes! With the strong our little girl, and that love gave us strength, And so did Haster Seals, Our entire family has worked closely with the experienced and compassionate staff.and volunteers. They helped us achieve all that she could: Rutherford people with disabilities across Fortunately, with the help of caring friends like you, there will always be Baster Seals, too. Easter Seals is renowned for providing the highest quality rehabilitative and support services for people of all ages who have disabilities -- helping them to uncover and develop their capabilities and full potential to contribute to society. Your gift now -- in as generous amount as your heart tells you to give -- will enable Baster Seals to continue to provide essential programs and services to men, wi Please take a minute to write out, omen and children with disabilities, a@ oheck and send it today. Believe me, your dollars go-a long way. Thank you so much for reading my story and for being a caring friend, PS. Your support can truly make Sincerely, Debbie Rutherford | a difference for families like ours. As little as $260 covers the annual cost for Haster Seals.to help one person achieve independence -- that's less than one dollar @ day. Please won't you show your support of people with disabilities by giving generously to our annual Easter Seal Campaign? PBBBA-A SEASIER SEALS: EASTER SEALS '90 ¥ Easter Seals THE FUND RAISING ARENA The Ultimate Tweak: Moore Response Hits a Grand Slam with the New Easter Seals Control In the world of direct mail, two techniques exist for beating con- trol: 1) imitative and 2) innovative. The imitative method means tweaking an existing control--improving the offer, adding a lift piece, changing the envelope, writing a new letter, or deleting an element to cheapen the CPM while maintaining the same allow- able CPO. Tweaking is traditionally done in-house; why hire a high-priced freelancer or agency to make what are essentially minor changes? Generally, individual tweaks will not result in any breakthrough, but they can raise response a percent or three with no increase in the cost of the mailing. Five such tweaks can give a to percent goose. After cycles of tweaking a control, it usually takes a work of genius to dethrone it. An innovative attempt to beat control means hiring a cre- ative team to do something completely different, something [|_| Eee tr mh ee a outside the box. Very few of these are successful, but mailers have to invest in these efforts. Which brings me to this extraordinary anomaly--the Easter Seals control that was tweaked and mailed by Moore Response with its awe- some inline printing capabilities. The advantages of a Moore Response package is that it can be made up of a myriad of personalized elements and mailed economically in the millions. It starts with a big roll of paper that can be printed in different typefaces and then sliced, trimmed, folded, and glued, whereupon you have a complete mailing with myr- iad elements personalized front and back: personalized outside enve- lope, personalized order form, personalized reply envelope, and personalized letter as well as other goodies inserted along the way, some also personalized, others in full color. As the late Dick Benson said, "Adding elements to a mailing package, even though obviously adding cost, is more likely to pay out than cheapening the package." The possible disadvantages of a Moore Response package are twofold: it achieves maximum efficiency when mailed in the mil- lions which means small tests can be horrendously expensive and sometimes all the personalization can be so overpowering that was obviously computer generated. As freelancer Bill Jayme said, "... in the marketplace as in theater, there is indeed a factor at work called 'the willing suspension of disbelief.'" Bill Cullen and Alan Gross of Moore Response went to Chris Cleghorn of Easter Seals and proposed they create a Moore Response version of its five-year control--same thrust, same letter, same sheet of Easter Seal stamps (which has been a tradition for years), but adding Moore's bells and whistles. Woabeatsbterst sens Maebrsatalatesndestlel THE FUND RAISING ARENA A Killer Fund Raiser The Amnesty International mailing you see here is, to say the least, unprepossessing at first glance. It arrives in a plain white #10 window envelope with the old- fashioned red-white-and-blue "airmail border" and a metered indi- cia. While the window does indicate that it is a "mass mailing," the metered indicia gives it a First Class look; the average consumer won't notice the postage is the non-profit rate of 9.3¢. On the back flap is simply the address: Eighth Avenue, New York, NY 10021. You have to open the envelope to see who this mailing comes from. The four-page letter--shown in its entirety on the page opposite--has no letterhead other than the Amnesty logo of a red candle with barbed wire snaking around it.
